title: TeamPCP Telnyx PyPI Supply Chain Attack: WAV Steganography Credential Stealer Targets 742,000+ Downloads in Escalating Multi-Package Campaign — Pivot to Vect Ransomware Monetization
affected_products: Any CI/CD pipeline installing telnyx>=4.87.1 between 03:51-10:13 UTC March 27, 2026

timeline.event: TeamPCP compromises Aqua Security Trivy scanner via misconfigured GitHub Actions; steals CI/CD secrets and PyPI publishing tokens
TeamPCP pivots to Checkmarx KICS using credentials stolen from Trivy; injects credential stealer into GitHub Actions workflows
TeamPCP compromises LiteLLM on PyPI (versions 1.82.7, 1.82.8) using stolen tokens; LiteLLM has 97M monthly downloads
03:51:28 UTC — TeamPCP publishes malicious telnyx 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 to PyPI using stolen credentials; WAV steganography payload deployed
10:13:00 UTC — Malicious telnyx packages quarantined and removed from PyPI after ~6.5 hour active window
TeamPCP announces collaboration with LAPSUS$ and Vect ransomware group; first named victim claim made; campaign enters monetization phase
No new package compromises reported; TeamPCP confirmed in active monetization phase, selling stolen credentials to Vect ransomware affiliate network

simple_summary.what_happened: A criminal hacking group called TeamPCP broke into the publishing accounts of the telnyx Python software package and secretly replaced two legitimate versions (4.87.1 and 4.87.2) with malicious ones containing hidden malware. The malware was disguised inside audio WAV files and stole passwords, cloud access keys, and SSH credentials from any developer's computer that downloaded these versions. The malicious packages were available for about 6.5 hours on March 27, 2026, before being removed. This is the fourth package in a campaign that started with the Trivy security scanner and has now pivoted to selling stolen credentials for ransomware attacks.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any developer or organization that installed or upgraded the telnyx Python package between 03:51 UTC and 10:13 UTC on March 27, 2026 (versions 4.87.1 or 4.87.2). With over 742,000 downloads and approximately 1 million monthly downloads, this includes software engineering teams, AI/cloud application developers, and CI/CD automated build pipelines that use Telnyx (a communications API platform). Organizations in telecom, SaaS, and cloud-native development are particularly at risk.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: TeamPCP steals PyPI publishing credentials from a previous supply chain victim (Trivy/LiteLLM) and uses them to upload modified telnyx package versions 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 containing hidden malicious code injected into telnyx/_client.py. Step 2: When any developer runs pip install telnyx, the malicious code executes automatically at import time — no explicit action required — and immediately connects to the attacker's command-and-control server at 83.142.209.203:8080. Step 3: The C2 server returns a WAV audio file (hangup.wav for Windows, ringtone.wav for Linux/macOS) that contains the actual malware payload encoded using steganography, disguising it as an audio file to evade security scanners. Step 4: On Windows, the malware drops a persistent executable as msbuild.exe in the Windows Startup folder so it runs every time the computer boots; on Linux/macOS, it immediately harvests credentials. Step 5: The malware collects SSH private keys, AWS/GCP/Azure cloud tokens, environment variables, database credentials, and API keys, encrypts them with AES-256-CBC and RSA-4096, and exfiltrates the encrypted bundle as tpcp.tar.gz via HTTP POST to the attacker. Step 6: Stolen credentials are passed to the Vect ransomware group's affiliate network, which uses them to deploy ransomware across the victim's cloud and enterprise infrastructure.
what_you_should_do: Immediately downgrade to telnyx==4.87.0 using: pip install telnyx==4.87.0 --force-reinstall, and verify no telnyx 4.87.1 or 4.87.2 is present in any environment

Assume all credentials are compromised if telnyx was upgraded on March 27 between 03:51-10:13 UTC: rotate ALL API keys, cloud tokens (AWS/GCP/Azure), SSH keys, GitHub tokens, database passwords, and PyPI publishing tokens
Check for the IOC msbuild.exe in %APPDATA%\...\Startup\ on Windows, and block/remove connections to 83.142.209.203:8080 at firewall level
Search all CI/CD pipelines, Docker images, and requirements files for telnyx>=4.87.1 and rebuild from clean base images rather than just upgrading packages
Pin all Python dependencies with hash verification (pip install --require-hashes) and pin GitHub Actions to full commit SHAs to prevent future supply chain attacks
Treat all credentials from March 19-27 as potentially compromised given the full TeamPCP campaign scope (Trivy → KICS → LiteLLM → Telnyx)
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This attack is serious for several reasons: First, the malware executes automatically on import, meaning developers may be infected just by installing a package update during routine work. Second, the use of WAV audio file steganography is a novel evasion technique that defeats most security scanners that look for executable code. Third, TeamPCP has now successfully compromised four widely-used developer tools in 8 days (Trivy, KICS, LiteLLM, Telnyx), demonstrating an accelerating supply chain attack cadence. Fourth and most critically, the campaign has evolved from intelligence gathering to active ransomware monetization through partnership with the Vect group — organizations whose credentials were stolen face imminent ransomware risk. Supply chain attacks of this sophistication have caused hundreds of millions in damages historically.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
tactics.description: TeamPCP compromised the telnyx PyPI package by using stolen publishing credentials to upload malicious versions containing credential-stealing code
Malware payload encoded within valid WAV audio container files using steganography, evading signature-based detection that focuses on executable file formats
Injected Python code in telnyx/_client.py executes at module import time using subprocess, os, and base64 modules to run the credential harvesting payload

tactics.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
tactics.description: On Windows, drops msbuild.exe (impersonating Microsoft Build Engine) into %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\ for persistence across reboots
Harvests SSH private keys, cloud access tokens (AWS/GCP/Azure), database credentials, and API keys from the filesystem and environment variables
Collects SSH keys, cloud tokens, credentials, and environment variables from local system before encrypting and exfiltrating
Credentials encrypted with AES-256-CBC + RSA-4096 and exfiltrated as tpcp.tar.gz bundle via HTTP POST to C2 server at 83.142.209.203:8080
Stolen credentials passed to Vect ransomware affiliate network for downstream ransomware deployment against victim organizations

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying the malicious package installation, C2 network connections to 83.142.209.203:8080, WAV file downloads from non-CDN sources, and Windows Startup folder persistence. Organizations should also audit Python package logs for telnyx 4.87.1/4.87.2 installation and check for credential exfiltration artifacts (tpcp.tar.gz).

log_sources.description: Monitor all outbound HTTP connections to 83.142.209.203 port 8080 and any HTTP GET requests for hangup.wav or ringtone.wav files

log_sources.source: Python Package Manager Logs / pip History
log_sources.description: Audit pip install history across all developer machines, CI/CD runners, and container build logs for telnyx==4.87.1 or telnyx==4.87.2
Monitor process creation from %APPDATA%\...\Startup\msbuild.exe and file creation in Startup folder
Scan all systems for tpcp.tar.gz files in /tmp, %TEMP%, or user home directories indicating successful credential exfiltration

log_sources.source: Cloud Audit Logs (AWS CloudTrail, GCP Audit, Azure Monitor)
log_sources.description: Monitor for unusual API calls, new IAM roles, or access from unexpected source IPs using credentials that could have been harvested on March 27
Review all build logs from March 27, 03:51-10:13 UTC for telnyx package installations; rebuild any affected containers or environments

what_to_look_for.description: TeamPCP C2 server used for WAV payload delivery and credential exfiltration; any connection confirms infection

what_to_look_for.indicator: HTTP GET requests for hangup.wav or ringtone.wav
what_to_look_for.description: These are the obfuscated malware payloads; any download of these specific files from the C2 server confirms active malware execution
Malware drops persistence mechanism masquerading as Microsoft Build Engine in user Startup folder; verify file legitimacy by path and digital signature
Encrypted credential bundle created immediately before exfiltration; presence confirms credential theft occurred

what_to_look_for.indicator: telnyx version 4.87.1 or 4.87.2 in installed packages
what_to_look_for.description: Direct indicator of exposure; check pip freeze, requirements.lock, container image layers, and virtual environments

what_to_look_for.indicator: subprocess spawning from Python telnyx module at import time
what_to_look_for.description: Malicious code executes subprocesses during telnyx module import; legitimate libraries do not spawn subprocesses on import

immediate_actions.description: Remove malicious versions 4.87.1 and 4.87.2 from all environments and pin to last safe version 4.87.0
Block all outbound connections to 83.142.209.203:8080 at network perimeter to stop active credential exfiltration and cut C2 communication

immediate_actions.action: Audit all environments for malicious package installation
immediate_actions.description: Search all developer machines, CI/CD systems, Docker images, and Kubernetes pods for evidence of telnyx 4.87.1 or 4.87.2 installation
immediate_actions.command: pip list 2>/dev/null | grep -i telnyx; find / -name '*.txt' -exec grep -l 'telnyx' {} \; 2>/dev/null

immediate_actions.action: Rotate ALL credentials from potentially affected systems
immediate_actions.description: Treat all credentials accessible from any environment where telnyx was installed as compromised. Immediately rotate: Telnyx API keys, AWS/GCP/Azure tokens, SSH keys, GitHub PATs, database passwords, PyPI tokens, Docker registry credentials
Search for msbuild.exe in Windows Startup folder across all Windows developer machines; remove immediately if found

immediate_actions.action: Rebuild affected CI/CD pipelines from clean images
immediate_actions.description: Credential rotation alone is insufficient if systemd backdoors were installed; rebuild all CI/CD runners from clean base images, regenerate all signing keys, and audit GitHub Actions workflows for injected steps
